coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-based absorbance device extended to ultraviolet (365 nm) and near infrared (780 nm) regions using ratiometric fluorescence measurement
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaolan
Yu, Lei
Kang, Qi
Sun, Yan
Huang, Yan
Shen, Dazhong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we developed a smartphone-based portable absorbance device with the wavelength covered from the ultraviolet (365 nm) to near infrared (780 nm) regions, expanding the applications of visualized colorimetric analysis. In a three-channel cuvette, the absorbance cell was sandwiched between two fluorescence detection chambers. The intensities of incident light and transmitted light with respect to the absorbance cell were measured from the fluorescence intensity of the reference and measurement chambers, respectively. The as-measured absorbance obeys the Lambert-Beer law in the range of 0 ~ 1.0. By using red-emitting carbon quantum dots as the detection fluorophore, the determinations of folic acid by ultraviolet absorbance (at 365 nm), dissolved oxygen by visible absorbance (at 505 nm), and hypochlorous acid by near-infrared absorbance (at 780 nm) were demonstrated. The influence of the variation in the light source intensity on the absorbance was corrected by the ratiometric strategy. The portable absorbance device was applied for the field analysis of dissolved oxygen in environmental water samples, offering the advantages of portability, reliability and accuracy for field analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smartphone-based analytical device
Absorbance
Ratiometric fluorescence
Field analysis
Dissolved oxygen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of HOCl by near-infrared absorbance in portable device
Determination of DO in water samples by Mn (III)-EDTA method
Principle of absorbance measurement based on ratiometric fluorescence strategy
Red-emission CDs as the light intensity indicator for absorbance measurement
Analytical performance of the portable absorbance device in ultraviolet region
Influence of the variation in light source intensity on absorbance in the portable device
Determination hypochlorite by absorbance at 780 nm in the smartphone based device
Application in the determination of dissolved oxygen in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874083 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China ( ZR2018MB029 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Images of the smartphone-based absorbance measurement device (A) and schematic drawing of the principle for absorbance measurement (B). (1) light tight box, (2) rechargeable power source, (3) power switch, (4) light source, (5) light source wheel, (6) three-channel cuvette, (7) light eliminator, (8) wide angle lens, (9) smartphone measurement platform. Where I 0 and I are the intensities of the incident and transmitted lights, F 1 and F 2 the fluorescence intensities of the reference and measurement chambers, H R1 and H R2 the corrected brightness parameters of the reference and measurement chambers, respectively.

Images of the fluorescence measurement chamber (A), red brightness and absorbance (B) in FA solutions at different concentration. (a) red brightness of the fluorescence images, (b) absorbance measured by the portable device, (c) absorbance measured by the UV-1700 spectrophotometer. The error bars show the standard deviation of three parallel determinations.

Artificially adjusted working voltage of the LED flashlight (A), the red brightness in the measurement and reference fluorescence chambers (B), logarithm of the corrected brightness parameter ratio in the measurement and reference fluorescence chambers and their difference (C). The solution in the absorbance cell was ultrapure water.

Images of the fluorescence measurement chamber (A), red brightness and absorbance calculated by Eq. (10) (B) in NaOCl solutions at different concentration. The blank solution did not contain the probe of Cy-HOCl. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three parallel determinations.
Comparison the concentration of DO in water samples determined by Winkler titration and Mn(III)-EDTA spectrometric methods measured by the developed smartphone-based device and lab spectrophotometer.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * Data were obtained in field analysis. Sampling date: 2020/08/27

head.ce:title: A smartphone-based absorbance device extended to ultraviolet (365 nm) and near infrared (780 nm) regions using ratiometric fluorescence measurement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in Universities of Shandong, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Peninsula Engineering Research Center of Comprehensive Brine Utilization, Weifang University of Science and Technology, Weifang 262700, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Peninsula Engineering Research Center of Comprehensive Brine Utilization
Weifang University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Peninsula Engineering Research Center of Comprehensive Brine Utilization, Weifang University of Science and Technology, Weifang 262700, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we developed a smartphone-based portable absorbance device with the wavelength covered from the ultraviolet (365 nm) to near infrared (780 nm) regions, expanding the applications of visualized colorimetric analysis. In a three-channel cuvette, the absorbance cell was sandwiched between two fluorescence detection chambers. The intensities of incident light and transmitted light with respect to the absorbance cell were measured from the fluorescence intensity of the reference and measurement chambers, respectively. The as-measured absorbance obeys the Lambert-Beer law in the range of 0 ~ 1.0. By using red-emitting carbon quantum dots as the detection fluorophore, the determinations of folic acid by ultraviolet absorbance (at 365 nm), dissolved oxygen by visible absorbance (at 505 nm), and hypochlorous acid by near-infrared absorbance (at 780 nm) were demonstrated. The influence of the variation in the light source intensity on the absorbance was corrected by the ratiometric strategy. The portable absorbance device was applied for the field analysis of dissolved oxygen in environmental water samples, offering the advantages of portability, reliability and accuracy for field analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid improvement in hardware and software, a smartphone is analogous to a mini computer with high-performance camera, USB port, Bluetooth, and ambient light sensor, promoting the development of the portable analytical sensing systems for care-of-point and field applications [1–3]. Due to the universality and multifunctional properties of smartphones, researchers have been actively engaged in developing potential analysis terminals for conducting numerous optical sensors. A smartphone-based analytical device offers the remarkable advantages in portability, low power consumption, cheapness, and convenience in location and data exchange for field analysis [4,5]. Typical examples include the visual colorimetric detection [6–9], fluoremetry [10–13], microscopy [14] chemical education [15,16], as well as data analysis and control interfaces [17,18], and so on. Among them, visual analysis is rapidly developed due to its simplicity and the extensive applications of colorimetric methods. Usually, the intensity of transmission light or hue of the test solution are detected by the built-in camera in the smartphone platforms [6–9,19–22]. In addition, the intensity of transmission light is also detected by the ambient light sensor in the smartphone in colorimetric methods [3,23].

It should be noted that the intensity of transmission light or hue measured in the colorimetric devices do not obey Lambert-Beer law. On the other hand, a light source with high stability is needed because the intensity or hue of transmission light depend on the intensity of the incident light source. However, based on the consideration of cost and portability for field analysis, simple light sources, such as LED lamp, flashlight, even ambient light, were usually used [23–30]. Thus, the drift in light intensity may be unavoidable in such portable devices, due to the variation in battery power consumption as well as the ambient temperature.

Herein, we reported a smartphone-based absorbance device for portable field analysis. By using a three-channel cuvette with the absorbance cell sandwiched between the reference and measurement fluorescence chambers, the fluorescent images of the detection fluorophore solutions were captured by the built-in camera. As shown in Fig. 1 , the intensities of the incident and transmission lights are measured simultaneously from the brightness of the images of the fluorophore solutions in the reference and measurement fluorescence chambers. Because influence of the variations in light source intensity and shooting parameters of the camera on the absorbance is suppressed by the ratiometric fluorescence strategy [31] the portable absorbance device offers the similar baseline stability as a double-beam spectrophotometer. By using red emission carbon quantum dots (r-CDs) as the detection fluorophore, the absorbance in ultraviolet, visible and near infrared regions are measured, expanding the applications of smartphone-based colorimetric analysis. As an example of the applicability, the smartphone-based colorimetric device was applied to determine dissolved oxygen (DO) in water samples on site.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without purification. Cetyltrimethyl ammoniun bromide (CTAB), folic acid and methyl orange (MO) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Manganese sulfate, p-phenylenediamine, sodium hypochlorite and disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA) were purchased from Aladdin Regent Company Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared using doubly-distilled water. The UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence spectra were measured on spectrophotometer (UV-1700, Shimadzu, Japan) and spectrofluorophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The morphological evaluation was examined on a transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-800, Hitachi, Japan).

The smartphone-based absorbance device is shown in Fig. 1, which includes mainly a smartphone (Honour 7, Huawei) and an optical detection module mounted in a light-tight box with the dimensions of 180 × 120 × 80 mm. To perform the absorbance measurements at different wavelengths, three light sources (365 nm LED flashlight, 505 nm and 780 nm laser pointers) were equipped. They were powered by a portable lithium battery source and switched by rotating the light source wheel. The light beam was perpendicular to the left wall of the three-channel quartz cuvette (10 × 10 × 45 mm of the inner wall for each chamber). The left and right detection chambers were filled with fluorophore solution to measure the intensities of the incident light (I 0) and transmitted light (I) with respect to the middle absorbance cell. The fluorescent images of the fluorophore solutions were captured by the built-in camera in smartphone with the help of an additional wide angle lens. The automatic functions of the camera were turned off during the photography. The averaged brightness of the red component in fluorescent images in reference (
R ¯
1
) and measurement (
R ¯
2
) areas were measured by the user-edited APP software. To eliminate the influence of the stray light, the non-detection area in the cuvette was blocked by black self-adhesive film and a black box was used as light eliminator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HOCl by near-infrared absorbance in portable device
ce:section.ce:para: The r-CDs were used as the light intensity detection fluorophore, which were prepared by a solvothermal method according to ref. [32] with slight modification. The details are described in the Electronic Supporting Materials (ESM). The near-infrared absorbance probe for HOCl (Cy-HOCl, structural formula shown in Scheme S1) was synthesized according to the method reported in a previous work [33]. The crude Cy-HOCl probe was purified by silica gel chromatography using ethyl acetate/methanol (4: 1, v/v) as eluent to obtain a green solid product. 2000 μL of NaOCl solution at different concentration, 500 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4, 0.015 M NaAc + 0.085 M HAc), and 500 μ L of Cy-HOCl solution (50 μM) were added into a 5 mL plastic centrifugal tube. The mixture was heated at 50 °C for 15 min in a water-bath then cooled to room temperature by tape water at ambient temperature. The total volume of the reaction mixture was diluted to 5.00 mL by distilled water. The mixture was transferred to the middle absorbance cell of the three-channel cuvette (Fig. 1A). By using a 780 nm laser pointer as the light source, the absorbance of the mixture was measured for the determination of HOCl by the portable device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DO in water samples by Mn (III)-EDTA method
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, a group specifically designed reaction tubes (Fig. S1) were used for DO determination on site. The reaction tube included a 1 mL inner tube fixed in a 10 mL outside tube. A hole (diameter of 4 mm) was drilled in the cap of the outside tube and sealed by 704 silicone gel layer. Prior to determination, 100 μL of 4 M NaOH solution was added in the inner tube, 40 μL of 0.1% CTAB and 80 μL of 3 M MnSO4 solutions were added in the outside tube. The reaction tubes were placed in a vacuum desiccator with the tube caps opened and vacuumized for 5 min to deoxygenate. Then nitrogen stream was introduced into the vacuum desiccator and the reaction tubes were sealed by the caps in the presence of nitrogen stream. At the sampling sites, water sample was collected with a sampling bottle of 500 mL. Immediately, 8 mL of water sample was drawn by a syringe and injected into the reaction tube via the silicone gel plughole. After a mixing time of 1 min, 0.2 g EDTA and 40 μL 5 M H2SO4 were added. The reaction mixture was shaken again then transferred into the middle absorbance cell in the three-channel cuvette. By using a 505 nm laser pointer as the light source, the absorbance of the mixture at 505 nm was measured for DO determination by the portable device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of absorbance measurement based on ratiometric fluorescence strategy
ce:section.ce:para:
When a beam of parallel monochromatic light (at wavelength of λ) is passed through a test solution, according to the Lambert-Beer law, the absorbance (A) is calculated by Eq. (1).
(1)
A = - lg T = lg
I 0
I
= ε b
C x
where T is the transmissivity, I 0 and I are the intensities of the incident and transmitted lights, ε, b and Cx are the molar absorption coefficient, length of absorption optical path and concentration of the light absorption species, respectively.

In a normal spectrophotometer, the light intensities in the reference and sample solutions are measured as the values of I 0 and I for the calculation of absorbance according to Eq. (1), which is performed separately on a single-beam spectrophotometer or simultaneously on double-beam spectrophotometer with two independent optic paths. Compared with a single-beam spectrophotometer, a double-beam spectrophotometer offers a much better baseline stability because the influence of the variation in light source intensity on I 0 and I is corrected in time.

In the smartphone-based device in Fig. 1, a three-channel cuvette was used to measure the absorbance via the ratiometric fluorescence strategy. The sample solution was added in the absorbance cell (middle) while detection fluorophore (r-CDs) solutions were added in the reference (left) and measurement (right) fluorescence chambers. When a parallel monochromatic light beam was passed through the cuvette, the fluorescence intensities of fluorophore solutions in the reference (F 1) and measurement (F 2) chambers were measured as the indicators for the I 0 and I of the absorbance cell (Fig. 1 B), respectively. In the case of dilute fluorophore solutions (A < 0.05), the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the intensity of the excitation source. Hence, we have
(2)
F 1
=
I 0
K
C 1
(3)
F 2
= I K
C 2
(4)
A = lg
I 0
I
= lg
F 1
F 2
- lg
C 1
C 2
= ε b
C x
where C 1 and C 2 are the concentrations of the fluorophore in the reference and measurement fluorescence chambers, K is a proportionality factor related to the molar absorption coefficient and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the fluorophore, respectively.

Because the images of the reference and measurement fluorescence chambers are recorded in the same picture, the proposed ratiometric fluorescence strategy for absorbance measurement offers theoretically the similar baseline stability as a double-beam spectrophotometer. However, as reported in previous papers [34,35], the correlation between the brightness of fluorescence image and the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore solution is non-linear. Hence, the as-measured red brightness of r-CDs was used only as the primary fluorescence signal. The following linearization is used to correct the influence of brightness saturation of a fluorescence image [31].
(5)
H
R 1
=
R ¯
1
-
R 10
255 -
R ¯
1
= k
F 1
(6)
H
R 2
=
R ¯
2
-
R 20
255 -
R ¯
2
= k
F 2
where H R1 and H R2, R 10 and R 20 are the corrected brightness parameters, background brightness of the red component in the fluorescence images of the reference and measurement chambers, k is the proportionality factor related to the sensitivity of spectrofluorometer and the exposure time and photosensibility of the charge coupled device (CCD) in camera, respectively. It should be noted that such linear transformation is valid in the case with
R ¯
1
< 220 and
R ¯
2
< 220, because the measurement error in H R is enlarged increasingly when
R ¯
is approached to the maximum of 255. Thus, the absorbance in the proposed smartphone-based device is calculated by Eq. (7).
(7)
A = lg
H
R 1
H
R 2
- lg
C 1
C 2
= lg
R ¯
1
-
R 10
255 -
R ¯
1
- lg
R ¯
2
-
R 20
255 -
R ¯
2
- lg
C 1
C 2
= ε b
C x

Because of the light absorption by the solvent as well as the light intensity loss from the walls of cuvette, the transmissivity of the blank solution is usually adjusted artificially to make T = 100% in absorbance measurement. Such adjustment is convenient in normal spectrophotometer via the magnification adjustment in the detection electronic, especially in a double-beam spectrophotometer. As the fluorescence intensity is usually proportional to the concentration of fluorophore in certain range, T = 100% in blank solution can be obtained by adjusting the concentration ratio of C 1/C 2 to make H R2 = H R1. But the adjustment of H R2 = H R1 (T = 100%) via the concentration of fluorophore is not convenient in experimental operation. In addition, the calculation of absorbance by Eq. (7) is also somewhat complicated as both H R1/H R2 and C 1/C 2 are needed. To overcome such shortcomings, we simplify the absorbance measurement in the smartphone-based device.

As mentioned above, the absorbance of a sample solution is referenced to the zero point level of the blank solution (T = 100%). That is to say, the absorbance of sample solution is equivalent to the absorbance difference between sample and the blank solutions. Accordingly, we have the following relations in the smartphone-based device in Fig. 1.
(8)
A 0
= - lg
T 0
= lg
H
R 10
H
R 20
≠ 0
in
blank
solution
(9)
A 1
= - lg
T 1
= lg
H
R 1
H
R 2
= ε b
C x
in
sample
solution
(10)
A =
A 1
-
A 0
= lg
H
R 20
H
R 2
- lg
H
R 10
H
R 1
= ε b
C x
where A 0 and A 1, T 0 and T 1, are the absorbance and transmissivity in the blank and sample solutions, H R10 and H R20 are the correct brightness parameters of the reference and measurement fluorescence chambers with the absorbance chamber filled blank solution, respectively.

As can be seen from Eq. (10), the absorbance in sample solution can be measured without the adjusting of T 0 = 100% or knowing C 1/C 2. Thus, only fixed concentrations of the fluorophore in the two fluorescence chambers are needed, offering the convenience in the experimental operation and calculation of absorbance.

In a commercial lab spectrophotometer, a continuous light source (for example, deuterium lamp in ultraviolet region and halogen lamp in visible and near-infrared regions) is usually used to provide monochromatic light at required wavelength with the help of monochromator and slit. Thus, the absorption spectrum can be measured by continuously scanning the absorbance with wavelength. But in the portable absorbance device shown in Fig. 1, only the absorbance at three wavelengths can be measured, which can be applied in the determination of limited analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Red-emission CDs as the light intensity indicator for absorbance measurement
ce:section.ce:para: In the portable absorbance device shown in Fig. 1, the two fluorescence chambers are serviced as the light intensity detectors. The photosensibility of this device is related to the molar absorption coefficient and PLQY of the fluorophore, as well as the shooting parameters of the camera. The available wavelength range depends on the emission wavelength of the light source and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of the fluorophore. In this work, a kind of red emission CDs was chosen as the detection fluorophore, which was synthetized from p-phenylenediamine by a solvothermal method [32]. As shown in Fig. S2A, the average particle size of the r-CDs is about 7.7 ± 0.9 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the r-CDs exhibits a strong peak around 284 nm and a broad peak centered at 518 nm (curve a in Fig. S2B). The fluorescence excitation spectrum of the r-CDs covers the wavelength range from 320 nm to 560 nm, with two peaks around 363 nm and 489 nm (curve b in Fig. S2B). As reported by Jiang et al. [32] the r-CDs also exhibit up-conversion fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 800 nm. Hence, the available wavelength range in this device can be extended to ultraviolet and near-infrared regions. In addition, carbon quantum dots as fluorescent materials have stronger photobleaching resistance than fluorescent dyes, increasing the service time of the fluorophore solutions.

As can be seen in Fig. S3, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the r-CDs exhibits a single peak with the full width at half-maximum of 51 ~ 57 nm. Similar to most of the carbon nanomaterials, the fluorescence emission peak of r-CDs is related slightly to the excitation wavelength. For example, the fluorescence emission peak was red-shifted from 597 nm to 617 nm with the excitation wavelength increased from 365 nm to 550 nm. The fluorescence image of the r-CDs shows orange-red and distributes mainly in the wavelength region of primary red component (560–700 nm) in RGB model [36]. Hence, the red brightness of the fluorescence image of the r-CDs is measured as the primary fluorescence intensity signal indicator for the absorbance measurements by the user-edited APP software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the portable absorbance device in ultraviolet region
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, the visualized analysis methods, including smartphone-based colorimetric devices, are based on the change in color or hue of the test solutions or films. To test the analytical performance of smartphone-based absorbance device in ultraviolet region, the standard solution of folic acid (FA) was used as the model analyte. FA is also named as vitamin B9, plays an important role in metabolism and is mandatory for the biosynthesis of purines, pyrimidines and methionine [37]. FA deficiency or excessive intake will lead to the occurrence of some diseases. Adequate intake of FA can prevent neural tube developmental defects during pregnancy [38]. FA solution is colorless and stable in weak alkaline condition. As shown in Fig. S4, FA solution exhibits two broad absorption bands with absorption maxima at 283 nm and 362 nm, respectively. Usually, the maximum absorption peak at 283 nm is used in the determination of FA for higher sensitivity [39]. Based on the considerations for portability in field analysis, the proposed device did not equip with continuous light source and monochromer, which are the essential parts in a normal lab spectrophotometer. As an alternative, a commercially available LED flashlight with the emission wavelength centered at 365 nm was chosen as the light source for the determination of FA. As shown in Fig. 2 A, with increasing FA concentration, the fluorescence image of r-CDs in the measurement chamber was changed from orange-red to dark red. Importantly, the as-measured absorbance by the portable device obeys the Lambert-Beer law in the range of 0 ~ 1.0 (r2 = 0.9989, curve b in Fig. 2B). Under the experimental conditions used, the slope of the calibration curve for FA is 0.08296 L mg−1, which is close to that (0.08405 L mg−1) obtained in UV-1700 spectrophotometer (curve c in Fig. 2B). But the error bars in the absorbance in the portable device are about 2.1 times of that obtained in UV-1700 spectrophotometer.

Interestingly, the red brightness of the images of the fluorescence measurement chamber was decreased near-linearly with increasing concentration of FA (r2 = 0.9961, curve a in Fig. 2B). This near-linear correlation can be explained below.

Under the condition of constant light source intensity, there is lg(H R10/H R1) = 0 in Eq. (10). In addition, the value of H R20 is fixed. Thus, Eq.(10) can be simplified by
(11)
lg
H
R 2
= lg
H
R 20
- ε b
C x

Mathematically, ln x can be expressed by Taylor series in Eq. (12).
(12)
ln x = 2
x - 1
x + 1
+
1 3
x - 1
x + 1
3
+
1 5
x - 1
x + 1
5
+ …
( x > 0 )

By using lnx ~ 2(x−1)/(x + 1), Eq. (11) can be simplified as
(13)
2
2.303
H
R 2
- 1
H
R 2
+ 1
≈ lg
H 0
- ε b
C x

According to the definition of H R2 in Eq. (6), Eq. (13) is rearranged in the following form.
(14)
R ¯
2
≈
255 +
R 20
2
+
( 146.8 - 0.5758
R 20
)
lg
H
R 20
-
( 146.8 - 0.5758
R 20
)
ε b
C x

It is reasonable that the brightness was decreased near-linearly with increasing concentration of FA in low concentration region. Noted that Eq. (14) can also explain the near-linear correlations between the light intensity or hue of the colorimetric images and concentration of target analytes in visible regions [23,24,28,40–45], which are also due to the non-linear response of the CCD in the built-in camera in a smartphone to light intensity. But the value of
R ¯
2
is related to the intensity of the light source (lgHR2
0), shooting parameters of camera, and the concentration of the fluorescent fluorophore. As a result, the absorbance is a better signal indicator than
R ¯
2
in quantitative determination by using the smartphone-based colorimetric portable device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the variation in light source intensity on absorbance in the portable device
ce:section.ce:para: As a cheap light source equipped in a portable absorbance device, the intensity of the LED flashlight or laser pointers is expected to decay with increasing service time due to the rising in temperature of the light sources and the consuming the power capacity of buttery. To straightway demonstrate such influence, we enlarge the variation in the intensity of the LED flashlight by adjusting artificially its working voltage. As shown in Fig. S5, besides the decrease due to the light absorption by FA molecules at higher concentration, the red brightness of the images in the measurement fluorescence chamber is decreased near-linearly with decreasing working voltage. In the five measurement cycles for calibration curves, the operating voltage of the LED flashlight was lowered artificially from 3.80 V to 3.60 V at the step of 50 mV (by a DC regulated power supply), resulting in the decrease in the initial red brightness in blank solution from 189.2 to 163.1 (curves a to e). The reason is that the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the intensity of the excitation light source, which is decreased with the declining working voltage. However, by using the absorbance as the signal indicator, the as-obtained calibration curves in the five measuring cycles are very close, indicating that the variation in light source intensity has little influence on absorbance (curve f). In addition, there is A = 0 in blank solution even without zero adjustment operation, which supports the analysis from Eq.(10).

The influence of the variation in light source intensity on the baseline was also tested. In this experiment, the intensity of the LED flashlight was also adjusted artificially via its working voltage (Fig. 3 A). It can be seen that the variation tendency of the red brightness of the images in the reference and measuring fluorescence chambers is very similar (Fig. 3B). By using the corrected brightness parameter in the first point as the reference, the values of lg(H R0/H R) are near the same in the reference and measuring fluorescence chambers (Fig. 3C). As a result, the absorbance, i.e. the difference of lg(H R20/H R2) −lg(H R10/H R1), is close to zero during the adjustment in the intensity of the LED flashlight. This result indicates again the good stability of the absorbance measured by the self-correction in the ratiometric fluorescence strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination hypochlorite by absorbance at 780 nm in the smartphone based device
ce:section.ce:para: As most of the molecules have no or only weak light absorption in the near-infrared region, the spectrometric determination based on the absorbance in near-infrared region usually offers better selectivity than in the UV–vis one. As an example to extend the application of smartphone-based absorbance device in near-infrared region, the determination hypochlorous acid (HOCl) by the Cy-HClO probe is chosen as the model. Hypochlorite acid is one of the biologically prominent reactive oxygen species which plays an essential part in immune defense against various bacteria in living innate immune system [46]. Hypochlorite acid is synthesized from the reaction of hydrogen peroxide with chloride ions catalyzed by the enzyme myeloperoxidase [47]. In addition, HOCl is also produced from the chlorination in water via the reaction with water and chlorine or from the use of NaOCl serving as a disinfectant [48]. There is growing evidence that an overproduction of HOCl could be responsible for some diseases, including arthritis, neurodegenerative disorders and cancer [49].

As shown in Scheme S2, the response unit of 4-amino-3-nitrophenol group in the Cy-HOCl probe can be specifically eliminated by HOCl. The oxidative cyclization reaction results in the decrease of the absorption peak at 780 nm in the near-infrared region (Fig. S6). As illustrated in Fig. S7, when the probe was incubated with the possible interfere species, such as glucose, cysteine, hydrogen peroxide, uric acid, glutathione, ascorbic acid, dopamine, and tyrosine, the absorbance of Cy-HOCl at 780 nm was influenced little. On the other hand, NaOCl resulted in remarkable decrease in the absorbance of Cy-HOCl probe in infrared region, exhibiting an acceptable selectivity toward HOCl.

According to the absorption spectrum of Cy-HOCl probe in Fig. S6, a 780 nm laser pointer was used in the portable device as the light source to measure the absorbance of Cy-HOCl. The intensities of the incident and transmitted lights were measured by the up-conversion fluorescence of r-CDs [32]. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence image of r-CDs in the measurement chamber was changed from dark red to orange-red. The red brightness was increased near-linearly with increasing NaOCl concentration due to fading reaction of Cy-HOCl by HOCl (Fig. 4B). The absorbance at 780 nm was decreased linearly with increasing NaOCl concentration from 0 to 12.5 μM. The slope of the calibration curve is −4.677×104 M−1, which is close to that (−4.709×104 M−1) measured in a UV-1700 spectrophotometer (Insert in Fig. S6). Based on the rule of 3σ, the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 0.73 μM in the portable absorbance device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in the determination of dissolved oxygen in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the applicability of the portable absorbance device, dissolved oxygen in water samples were determined on site. Dissolved oxygen in water is one of the cornerstones of water ecosystems, because DO is the essential substance for aquatic animals to survive. Usually, DO in water is an indicator of the self-purification ability of a water system. The classical method for DO determination in water samples is iodometry, i.e. Winkler titration [50,51]. Other methods, such as photometric titration [52] electrochemical method [53–55] colorimetric method [56–59], fluorescent probes [60,61] are also reported for DO determination in water samples.

In this work, a Mn(III)-EDTA colorimetic method was used to determine DO in water sample on site. The major reactions are described below.
Mn2+ + 2 OH– → Mn(OH)2 ↓
O2 + 4Mn(OH)2 ↓ + 2H2O → 4Mn(OH)3↓
Mn(OH)3 ↓ + EDTA + 3H+ → Mn(III)-EDTA + 3H2O

As shown in Fig. S8, Mn(III)-EDTA exhibits broad absorption peak in the wavelength in the range from 400 to 650 nm. The absorbance at 505 nm is increased with increasing concentration of DO in the range of 0 ~ 9.2 mg L−1. Hence, a 505 nm laser pointer was used as the excitation and measurement light source in the portable device. As can be seen in Fig. S9, the brightness of the images was reduced near-linearly with increasing concentration of DO. The slope of the calibration curve in absorbance in the portable device is 0.04403 L mg−1, which is close to that (0.04476 L mg−1) measured in a lab spectrophotometer (Insert in Fig. S8).

It should be noted that oxygen in air is a potential interference source in the determination of DO, especially in water samples at low concentration of DO. It is important to avoid the dissolution of O2 from air into solutions during the sample and measurement stages. For the convenience to prepare the standard solution of DO in field analysis, we use MO solution (pH = 3.0) as the indirect standard for DO. As can be seen from Fig. S10, the absorption spectrum of MO solution (pH = 3.0) is well overlapped with that of Mn(III)-EDTA. According to the slope ratio of the calibration curves of DO to MO, 1 mg L−1 MO (pH = 3.0) is equivalent to 0.5401 mg L−1 DO. We performed the field analysis of DO in real water samples at five sites (Jinan city, Shandong province). As listed in Table 1 , DO concentration measured by the portable device on site is well agreed with that determined by the classic Winkler titration method.

In this work, we demonstrated that a smartphone-based portable device can measure the absorbance in the ultraviolet, visible and near-infrared region. By using the ratiometric fluorescence strategy, the baseline drift due to the variation in excitation light intensity is suppressed significantly, which is helpful to improve the stability and reliability of the simple portable detection device. The absorbance measured by the smartphone-based portable device is closed to that obtained by a lab spectrophotometer. This cheap absorbance device offers the advantages of portability, reliability and accuracy for field analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874083) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2018MB029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.105978.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
